senses.definition: Having or furnished with a slipcase (a protective case around a book or object).
examples: A slipcased edition of the novel sits on the shelf.
The slipcased set arrived in pristine condition.

senses.definition: Protective cases, usually cardboard or similar material, used to encase books or magazines.
examples: The slipcases protect the books during transport.
Collectors stored their editions in decorative slipcases.

senses.definition: A railway carriage that is detached from the rear of a moving train to serve intermediate destinations; an older practice now largely historical.
examples: The slipcoach was uncoupled at the next station to continue to a smaller town.
Rail enthusiasts study the era of slipcoaches as a curious chapter in train operation.
collocations: slipcoach at the end of the train

senses.definition: A coating or finish applied to surfaces to prevent sticking or buildup; used in industrial contexts.
examples: The factory applied a slipcoat to reduce material adhesion on equipment.
Maintenance used slipcoat to keep parts clean during processing.

senses.definition: A brand or generic term for a protective non-stick coating used on equipment to prevent buildup.
examples: Slipcote reduces clogging in hoppers and conveyors.
Agricultural facilities use Slipcote to minimize residue.

senses.definition: A cover that slips over a cushion, pillow, or furniture to protect or decorate it.
examples: She bought a slipcover to refresh the sofa.
The cotton slipcover is easy to wash.

senses.definition: Covered with a removable slipcover.
examples: The slipcovered chair looked fresh and clean.
They kept the slipcovered sofa in the living room.

senses.definition: The act of fitting or providing a slipcover; or the slipcover itself when in use.
examples: Replacement slipcovering updated the room's look.
Slipcovering can extend the life of old furniture.

senses.definition: Removable coverings for furniture to protect or change appearance.
examples: Several slipcovers were available in different colors.
They prefer slipcovers to keep the sofa clean.

senses.definition: A woman's lightweight dress worn as a layering piece or outerwear in dressy contexts.
examples: She wore a satin slipdress to the party.
The slipdress was paired with a blazer for a chic look.

senses.definition: Plural of slipdress; multiple slipdresses.
examples: Her closet holds several slipdresses for different occasions.
Slipdresses can be worn with sneakers for a casual look.

senses.definition: To slide or cause to slide; archaic/rare variant of slip.
examples: The stone slipe along the bank.
They slipe the cart down the hill carefully.

senses.definition: Past tense of slip; moved accidentally or lost footing.
examples: He sliped on the wet floor and fell.
The boat slipped its moorings during the storm.

senses.definition: Plural of slip; a slip or error; or a thin film; or a type of soft fabric (less common).
examples: He made several slips in his calculation.
The slip of the tongue caused a awkward moment.

senses.definition: A form used in concrete construction that slides to create a vertical surface as concrete sets.
examples: The wall rose as the slipform advanced.
Engineers monitored the slipform to ensure straight edges.

senses.definition: Built or created using a slipform technique.
examples: A slipformed column reached full height.
Slipformed walls appeared seamless.

senses.definition: The process of constructing using a slipform method.
examples: Slipforming is used for tall concrete structures.
The crew focused on precise slipforming operations.

senses.definition: Multiple slipform structures or units used in construction.
examples: Two slipforms operate in tandem on the project.
Slipsforms enable continuous concrete casting.

senses.definition: A historical device, a gibbet, used for hanging, with a 'slip' element as part of its mechanism in some contexts; mostly archaic/lant language.
examples: References to the slipgibbet appear in old ballads.
The term is rarely used in modern English.

senses.definition: An obsolete or specialized term possibly referring to a fastening or supporting piece; not common in modern usage.
examples: The antique device included a sliphalter component.
Scholarly glossaries list sliphalter among old hardware terms.

senses.definition: A horn attached to a slip or fastening mechanism in certain devices; largely rare or historical.
examples: The workshop catalog lists a sliphorn as a supplementary part.
Sliphorn is rarely used in contemporary technical writing.

senses.definition: Alternative spelling or variant form of sliphorn; refers to a small horn or signaling device in older equipment.
examples: Some manuals mention the sliphorn in old signaling systems.
The term appears mainly in historical descriptions.

senses.definition: A mis-spelling or less common form seen in some texts; see sliphorn.
examples: The catalog lists sliphorn as a variant spelling.
Cross-check spellings when researching old manuals.

senses.definition: A building or part of an industrial site where slipping or sliding operations occur; historically specific term.
examples: The sliphouse stored equipment used for slipforming.
Industrial reports mention the sliphouse in context of production lines.

senses.definition: The act of sliding or moving smoothly; can also mean the process of sliding or decoupling in rail contexts.
examples: The slipping of the train was carefully controlled.
In rail terms, slipping involves decoupling or separating parts.

senses.definition: A knot that can be easily tightened or loosened; used in sailing, climbing, or crafts.
examples: Tie a slipknot at the start of the rope.
The sailor adjusted the rope with a slipknot for quick release.

senses.definition: Plural of slipknot; multiple easily adjustable knots.
examples: The climber tied several slipknots for quick adjustments.
Use slipknots to fasten gear temporarily.
